segments.s509-g73: به شبگیر is a temporal prepositional phrase (به + شبگیر) meaning ‘toward/at nightfall’ and comes before the subject for emphasis. شاه یمن is the subject noun phrase ('the king of Yemen') with the modifier یمن following شاه as in Persian noun + modifier order. بازگشت is the past third-person singular of بازگشتن ('he returned') and is the main verb; Persian often omits an explicit pronoun, so بازگشت carries the action for the subject.
segments.s509-g74: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from' marking source or cause. لشکر جهانی is a noun + adjective sequence meaning 'the universal/entire host' (لشکر = host/army, جهانی = universal/whole). پر آواز is an adjectival phrase ('full of voice/voices' — i.e. filled with cries), where پر is 'full (of)' preceding the noun آواز. گشت is the literary past of گشتن/شدن meaning 'became' (turned into); together the clause says the host 'became full of voices' (i.e. resounded with shouts).
segments.s509-g41: گرفتند is a 3rd-person plural past tense (‘they seized/took’). زیشان is the fused prepositional phrase ز + ایشان = “from them / of them” and follows the verb here (word order: verb + source). فراوان modifies اسیر and is an adjective meaning “many / abundant,” so the clause reads literally “they took from them many captives.”
segments.s509-g42: This is a noun list enumerating the captives: زن “women” + و “and” + کودک “child” modified by خرد meaning “small” (here physical/age sense), then و + برنا “youthful/young” and و + پیر “old/elderly.” The coordinating و simply links items in a series; the adjectives خرد and برنا describe age/size.
segments.s509-g43: سوی نیمروز آمد is a prepositional phrase (سوی = “toward”) + place (نیمروز here as the region/area) followed by the past verb آمد “he came.” از راه بست pairs another prepositional phrase (از راه = “from/the road”) with the past verb بست “he closed,” so two past actions are narrated in sequence (came and closed the way). Note بست is the past tense of بستن used here as “closed.”
segments.s509-g44: همه روی گیتی is a poetic noun phrase: همه “all” + روی گیتی “the face/surface of the world.” ز is the poetic contracted form of از (“from/of”), marking the agent or source (here ‘of the enemy’). بشست is the past tense of شستن with the archaic/epic prefix ب‑ (3rd-person singular ‘he washed’), so the line means he ‘washed’ or cleansed the whole world of the enemy (i.e., wiped out/cleared enemies).
segments.s509-g45: وزان is a compressed classical phrase = و + از آن (literally “and/from that”), locating the action relative to what came before. جایگه “place/position” is the noun, and شد is the past tense of شدن; here شد functions to mark a change of state or the beginning of motion (‘set out’ or ‘was at / went from that place’) so the phrase means ‘and from that place he went toward…’. به سوی یمن is the direction phrase ‘toward Yemen.’
segments.s509-g46: This is a nominal description of accompaniment and status: جهاندار is a title ‘sovereign / ruler of the world.’ و simply links items. با introduces the company/means (‘with’), and نامدار انجمن is a genitival phrase meaning ‘renowned assembly / illustrious company’ — i.e. he went accompanied by noted nobles or an illustrious retinue.
segments.s509-g47: چو is the classical conjunction ‘when.’ بشنید is 3rd-person past ‘he heard.’ The sequence places the temporal clause before the subject: ‘when the king of Yemen heard…’ شاه یمن is the subject ‘the king of Yemen,’ and با مهان (‘with guests/retainers’) indicates he came accompanied by his men; مهان in epic poetry often means nobles/guests/attendants.
segments.s509-g48: بیامد is the past form with the prefix ب‑ meaning ‘he came.’ بر here means ‘to/toward’ (a directional dative), شهریار is ‘king/lord,’ and جهان is attached to شهریار as an epithet (‘king of the world’). The whole clause therefore reads ‘he came to the world‑king.’
segments.s509-g49: بسی هدیه‌ها is a topical noun phrase: بسی (poetic) quantifies هدیه‌ها (gifts) as 'many/much'. کز introduces a relative clause of origin ('which from Yemen'), and برگزید is a past-tense verb (3rd person singular) 'he chose/selected' closing that relative clause. Word order is noun phrase + relative clause + past verb, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s509-g50: This is an adjectival phrase and a short clause modifying the preceding gifts: بهاگیر and زیبا are adjectives applied to the gifts ('valuable' and 'beautiful'), joined by و. چنانچون is a comparative/relational particle 'just as/just so', and سزید is an archaic past-form of سزدن/سزایدن used here in the sense 'to befit / to deserve' (3rd person), meaning 'as was fitting'.
segments.s509-g51: The clause has numeral + noun (ده اشتر, 'ten camels') followed by a prepositional/ablative phrase ز برد یمن ('from the spoils/booty of Yemen'), and the compound verb بار کرد ('he loaded') in past tense. بار کردن here means 'to load (with cargo)'; ز indicates source or contents.
segments.s509-g52: دگر (here 'another') introduces the next group; پنج را uses را as a definite/direct-object marker on the (elliptical) 'five (camels)', and بار دینار کرد means 'loaded (them) with dinars' — بار + noun + کرد is a verbal construction 'to load with X'. The noun for 'five' (shutter/camels) is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s509-g53: Again a nominal phrase with دگر ('another') + numeral + noun (ده شتر, 'ten camels'), followed by the verb بار کرد ('he loaded') and the phrase از درم where از indicates the material/contents ('with/consisting of dirhams'). The verb is past tense (3rd person).
segments.s509-g54: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction here meaning 'when' or 'if'; باشد is an archaic/present-subjunctive copular form ('there be' / 'is'); درم is the noun 'dirham/money' as the subject of the clause. دل نباشد به غم is a negated predicate phrase meaning literally 'the heart is not in sorrow' — idiomatically, 'the heart has no sorrow.' The structure is a general conditional/proverbial statement (If there is money, the heart will not be sorrowful).
segments.s509-g55: دگر introduces an additional item; سله is 'a gift/parcel' and begins an appositional list of its contents. زعفران specifies saffron as one of the contents; the sequence زعفران بد هزار is poetic/elliptical and should be read as an emphatic quantity ('to the amount of a thousand [units] of saffron'). بد here is a poetic particle/word whose exact nuance is archaic or idiomatic and modifies the quantity phrase.
segments.s509-g56: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing further items: دیبا (brocade) and هرجامه ('every/each garment' or 'many garments') joined by و. بی‌شمار is an adjective meaning 'countless' or 'innumerable' and modifies the garments/brocades. The whole phrase lists luxuriant textiles as part of the gift.
segments.s509-g25: سر سندیان is a genitive phrase 'the head/leader of the Sindians' (noun + possessor). بود is the past copula 'was'. بنداه نام is a postnominal naming formula: بنداه is here a proper name (بنداه نام = 'called Bandah'), with نام ('name') placed after the name as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s509-g26: سواری is the noun 'horseman/rider' modified by the following adjective سرافراز (postpositive adjective = 'lofty/proud'). با governs the coordination رای و کام ('with counsel and desire') — an instrumental/qualifying phrase describing his disposition or authority.
segments.s509-g27: یکی is an indefinite 'one/someone'. رزمشان کرده شد is a verbal complex: کرده (perfect participle 'having done' / 'having engaged') + شد (passive/auxiliary 'was/made'), so the clause means 'one who had engaged in their battle was (so)'. همگروه is a predicate noun 'a fellow/comrade' or 'one of their group' and completes the passive construction.
segments.s509-g28: زمین شد is an idiomatic past construction 'became (the) ground' = 'fell to the ground'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of', and افگنده is a past participle 'thrown' (showing cause: 'having been thrown'). بر سان کوه is a simile 'upon/as if a mountain' (بر = on/upon, سان = like/as, کوه = mountain).
segments.s509-g29: شب آمد is a simple past 'night came/fell'. بران دشت is an adverbial phrase 'on that plain' (بران = 'on that/thereupon' + دشت 'plain'). سندی is used substantively 'the Sindhis' and نماند is past 'remained' — here meaning 'there remained none' (i.e. they were gone).
segments.s509-g30: سکندر is the subject. سپاه is 'army/host' and here names what he commands. از پس‌اندر is an adverbial phrase 'from behind/in pursuit', and براند (classical form of راندن) is a simple past or narrative present meaning 'drove (on)/pursued'. The clause means 'Alexander drove (his) army in pursuit.'
segments.s509-g31: به دست آمدش is an idiom 'came into his hand' = 'came into his possession' (به = to/into, دست = hand/possession, آمدش = 'came to him'). پیل هشتاد و پنج is a counted noun phrase 'eighty-five elephants' (pīl governs the numeral following it). The clause reports acquisition/booty.
segments.s509-g32: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' referring back to spoils; تاج زرین is a noun + adjective 'golden crown' (tāj + zarrīn). The coordinating و links شمشیر 'sword' and گنج 'treasure', listing the captured regalia and wealth.
segments.s509-g57: This noun phrase identifies a single cup made of the gem 'زبرجد'. Word order is noun (زبرجد) + numeral/indefinite (یکی) + head noun (جام). بودش is the past-formal fusion of بودن + enclitic ش meaning 'it was' (i.e. 'there was such a cup'); به گنج marks location/possession 'in the treasure/treasury'.
segments.s509-g58: همان ('that/the same') refers back to the cup. در + ناسفته is a prepositional phrase 'in (it) — ناسفته is an archaic participle meaning 'unworked/uncut'. The numeral هفتاد و پنج ('seventy and five') comes after and is connected by و used as the internal conjunction of the compound numeral.
segments.s509-g59: یکی جام دیگر = 'another/some other cup' (یکی as indefinite/one + noun + دیگر 'other'). بدش here is the archaic past-of-بودن (spelled بُد) plus enclitic ش meaning 'it was' — i.e. 'that cup was (made of)'. لاژورد names the material (lapis lazuli).
segments.s509-g60: نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed'. اندرو = اندر + او (in/inside it) 'in it'. شست here is ambiguous in the list but in classical Persian poetry it commonly represents the numeral 'sixty' (so read as a numeral + following noun); یاقوت زرد = 'yellow rubies'. The verb نهاد governs the whole phrase 'placed in it sixty yellow rubies'.
segments.s509-g61: ز (poetic form of از) introduces source 'from'. یاقوت سرخ = 'the red ruby'. از + برش 'from the cut/section' indicates 'from its facets/cuts', and ده نگین is the numeral 'ten gems' as the quantity taken/produced from that source. The clause omits an explicit verb here; the action is continued in the next segment.
segments.s509-g62: به فرمانبران داد — به marks the indirect object 'to/for the obedient (servants)', داد is the past 'gave'. و کرد آفرین is a light-verb construction: کرد 'did/uttered' + آفرین (an acclamation/exclamation 'bravo' / 'praise'); together 'and he praised/uttered acclamation'. The two verbs are sequential actions (gave ... and praised).
segments.s509-g63: به پیش = 'to the presence of'; سراپرده شهریار is a genitive construction 'the inner curtain/chamber of the king' (سراپرده = inner chamber/behind the curtain, شهریار follows as possessor). The prepositional phrase places the arrival in the king's presence.
segments.s509-g64: رسیدند is past plural 'they arrived/reached'. با هدیه و با نثار ('with gift(s) and with offering(s)') lists the things they brought; repetition of با ('with') is a stylistic/poetic parallelism.
segments.s509-g17: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when’. گفتار گوینده is a genitive construction ‘the speaker’s words/speech’ (noun + modifier). بشنید is past simple third-person singular ‘he heard’; in this poetic line the verb precedes the subject شاه (inversion common in verse): ‘when he heard the speaker’s words, the king…’.
segments.s509-g18: ز حلوان سوی سند is a sequence of prepositional/directional phrases: ز حلوان ‘from Ḥalwān’ and سوی سند ‘toward Sind’. شد is the past of شدن used here as the main intransitive past (‘turned/moved’ or ‘set out’) and governs the following adjunct با سپاه ‘with an army’ (با + noun = ‘with’). Word order is compact and somewhat inverted as is normal in epic verse.
segments.s509-g19: پذیره شد‌ندش is a passive/adjectival participle construction: پذیره (reception/received) plus شدند (past of شدن as auxiliary) with the enclitic pronoun -ش ‘him’ = ‘they received him’ / ‘he was received’. Syntactically the predicate appears before its subject, so سواران سند (‘the horsemen of Sind’) is the agent/subject following the verb phrase.
segments.s509-g20: همان جنگ را marks ‘that very battle’ (همان modifies جنگ) with the object marker را for emphasis; یاور آمد ز هند means ‘an ally/helper came from India’. The noun یاور is the subject (‘an ally’) and آمد is simple past ‘came’, while ز هند is the source complement ‘from India’.
segments.s509-g21: هرانکس که introduces a relative/generalizing clause ‘whoever’ / ‘anyone who’. که is the relative/complementizer; از فور ‘from the suddenness’ or ‘at once/at the onset’ (فور = immediately); دل‌خسته بود is a past-tense predication ‘was heart-weary’ (دل‌خسته = ‘weary/sorrowful in heart’, بود = past ‘was’).
segments.s509-g22: به خون ریختن is a verbal-noun phrase ‘(the) shedding of blood’ (به + infinitive/noun in Persian can mark the area/aim). دستها شسته بود is a past perfect-like construction: شسته (washed) + بود (auxiliary past) = ‘had washed his hands’. Together the clause means ‘had washed his hands with respect to/shunning the shedding of blood’ i.e. ‘had refrained from blood-shedding’.
segments.s509-g23: بردند is past tense ‘they carried/brought’. پیلان (plural of پیل) = ‘elephants’ is the direct object; و links the coordinated object هندی درای where هندی ‘Indian’ is modified by درای ‘possessing/wealthy’ (an epithet ‘wealthy Indians’). The verb precedes its objects here, a poetic ordering.
segments.s509-g24: خروش آمد is a nominal + past-verb idiom ‘a roar arose’ (خروش = ‘roar/uproar’, آمد = ‘came/arose’). The conjunction و links to ناله کرنای ‘the lament/moan of the trumpet’ where ناله (moan/lament) is governed by کرنای (trumpet, here in an attributive/genitive relationship). The second noun phrase is elliptically presented (the verb is implied from the first clause).
segments.s509-g33: A coordinated noun phrase listing groups: 'زن و کودک و پیر مردان' = 'women, children, and old men'. The phrase ends with the directional phrase 'به راه' where 'به' is the preposition indicating motion/toward and 'راه' is the noun 'road/way' — together 'on the road' or 'set out'. Word order is noun(s) + prepositional direction.
segments.s509-g34: 'برفتند' is the 3rd-person plural past form meaning 'they went/left' (an older/poetic motion verb). 'گریان' is a participial/adverbial form 'weeping', modifying the action. 'به نزدیک شاه' is a directional phrase 'toward/near the king' with 'به' marking direction, 'نزدیک' as 'near', and 'شاه' the destination.
segments.s509-g35: 'که‌ای' is an archaic/vocative particle addressing someone ('O ...'); here it introduces the direct address 'شاه بیدار' = 'alert/awake king', with the adjective following the noun (common in poetry). 'با رای و هوش' is an instrumental prepositional phrase 'with counsel and intellect' specifying means.
segments.s509-g36: 'مشور' here functions as an imperative/jussive 'consult / take counsel' (an older, terser verbal form). 'این بر و بوم' = 'this land/realm' (direct object/topic), where 'بر' is the preposition 'on/upon' used with 'بوم' ('homeland'). The second clause 'و بر بد مکوش' uses the negative imperative 'مکوش' = 'do not do/attempt' with 'بر بد' = 'upon (it) evil' — 'do not bring evil upon it.'
segments.s509-g37: A subordinate clause introduced by 'که' = 'that/for'. 'فرجام' = 'end/outcome' and 'هم' = 'also' modify 'روز' = 'day/period'; 'تو' is the 2nd-person subject. 'بگذرد' is the verb 'to pass' (3rd-person), here stating that the person's day/outcome will pass/come to an end.
segments.s509-g38: 'خنک آنک' is an idiomatic/poetic turn meaning roughly 'fortunate/good is that' or 'may it be that' (an evaluative adverbial + relative particle). 'گیتی' = 'the world' is the subject of the following verb. 'به بد نسپرد' = 'does not entrust/hand over to the bad' where 'نسپرد' (from سپردن) means 'entrust/hand over' and 'به بد' = 'to the bad/wicked'.
segments.s509-g39: 'سکندر' is the subject (Alexander). 'بریشان' is a prepositional phrase 'بر + ایشان' = 'to/for them' (direction/target). 'نیاورد' is the negative past 'did not bring', and 'مهر' is the object 'affection/kindness' — together 'Alexander did not bring them affection'.
segments.s509-g40: 'بران' (archaic/poetic) introduces the beneficiary/target 'بران خستگان' = 'to those weary ones'. 'هیچ' = 'not at all/none' intensifies the negation. 'ننمود' is the negative past 'did not show', and 'چهر' = 'face/appearance' — the phrase 'هیچ ننمود چهر' idiomatically means he showed them no face (no pity or attention).
segments.s509-g65: This clause has the proper name سکندر as subject followed by the literary past verb بپرسید ('he asked'). The coordinating conjunction و links to the next verb; بنواختشان is a past-form verb with the object clitic -شان ('them') attached and here means 'caused/let sound' (i.e. 'had music played for them').
segments.s509-g66: بر تخت نزدیک is a prepositional phrase: بر ('on/upon') + تخت ('throne') with the adjective/locative نزدیک ('near') following the noun (post-nominal). بنشاختشان is a literary past-form verb with the same object clitic -شان; in this context it means 'he recognised/perceived them' (literary sense), so the whole clause reads 'he perceived/recognised them near the throne.'
segments.s509-g67: آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction: آفرین (an exclamation 'bravo') + کرد ('did/made') → 'exclaimed/applauded'. برو here is an interjection/imperative 'go!' placed before the verb. The agent (شاه یمن, 'the king of Yemen') follows the verbal phrase in this inverted literary order.
segments.s509-g68: که introduces reported speech or an optative clause ('that' / 'may it be that'). پیروزگر is a predicate/adjectival noun 'victorious' and باش is the present/imperative form used optatively here ('be' → 'may you be'). بر انجمن is a prepositional phrase ('in/on the assembly' = 'before the gathering').
segments.s509-g69: به تو شادم is an idiomatic predication: به ('for/to') + تو ('you') + شادم ('I am glad'). ار is an archaic conditional 'if' (modern اگر). باشی is the present/subjunctive form 'you be/stay' (here dependent on the conditional), ایدر is an archaic locative 'here', and دو ماه follows as the time expression 'two months'.
segments.s509-g70: برآساید is a literary verb meaning 'to be at ease / be relieved' (third-person, here describing the result). از marks source ('from' or 'away from') and راه شاه و سپاه ('the road/way of the king and the army') is the phrase indicating what one is relieved from; the clause expresses that by staying two months one will be spared the king's-and-army's route/hardships.
segments.s509-g71: This repeats the exclamatory pattern: the name سکندر (subject) + the interjection برو آفرین (imperative/exclamation) + کرد ('did/exclaimed') and then و گفت ('and said'), with گفت marking the following reported speech. The sequence is standard literary narrative: action/exclamation followed by reported words.
segments.s509-g72: که introduces the quoted blessing/wish. با تو ('with you') + همیشه ('always') sets the adverbial frame. خرد ('wisdom') is the noun wished for; باد is the optative particle ('may it be'), and جفت ('companion, mate, pair') here means 'companion' — together: 'May wisdom always be your companion.'
segments.s509-g9: برفتند is a past-plural verb ('they went/they departed') placed before its accompanying prepositional phrases. با + noun is the common preposition 'with', repeated for emphasis: با هدیه و با نثار = 'with a gift and with an offering'. نثار here functions as a noun 'offering/tribute'.
segments.s509-g10: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' indicating source or content. حلوان here names sweetmeats (i.e. the kind of offerings). سران = 'the chiefs/notables' (subject/agents being carried or coming). تا ... در introduces the limit/place: تا = 'as far as/up to', در = 'in/at', شهریار = 'the king' (the destination: up to the king's presence).
segments.s509-g11: سکندر is the proper name 'Alexander'. سبک modifies پرسش as an adjective meaning 'light/trifling' (a brief or not weighty question). پرسش is the object 'question'; اندر گرفت is a verbal phrase 'took up' or 'seized upon' (گرفت used here as 'took up/posed').
segments.s509-g12: که introduces the indirect speech/what was asked ('that'/'namely'). ایدر = 'here'. چه is the interrogative 'what'. بینید is used as a present/interrogative verb 'do you see' (classical usage with -ید); it addresses the addressee(s). چیزی شگفت = 'something wondrous' (چیزی = 'something', شگفت = 'wonderful/marvelous').
segments.s509-g13: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (indirect object). گفت here functions as the past verb 'said' (word order inverted: indirect object before verb and subject). گوینده = 'the speaker/narrator'; کای is the vocative particle 'O' used before شهریار 'king' (address: 'O king').
segments.s509-g14: ندانیم is the first-person-plural present-negative form 'we do not know' (ن + دانیم). چیزی = 'anything/something'. که introduces a relative/content clause 'that/which'. آید is the present/subjunctive of آمدن 'come' used in the idiom آید به کار = 'be of use / come to (one's) hand'. به is the preposition 'to/for', and کار here means 'use/affair' — together meaning 'that will be of use'.
segments.s509-g15: برین is the fused form of بر + این = 'on/at this'. مرز = 'border/frontier'. درویشی denotes the condition 'dervishhood/poverty/ascetic life' and is treated as a noun; و links it to رنج 'hardship/suffering'. هست is the copula 'is/there is' placed at the end, a normal classical Persian order.
segments.s509-g16: کزین = 'from/if you pass this'. بگذری is a subjunctive/second-person singular form 'you pass' (in conditional-optative constructions). باد here is the optative particle 'may' (a wish/benediction), not the noun 'wind'. ماند = 'remain' (third-person past/stative); به + دست 'in (your) hand' is an idiom meaning 'in your possession/power'. The line expresses a wish/blessing on passing the hardship.
segments.s509-g1: بدان جایگه = a prepositional temporal/location phrase: بد-ان functions as 'to/at that' (classical contraction of به + آن) followed by جایگه 'place'. شاه is the subject 'the king' and ماهی here is an adverbial/time-word 'for a month' (classical/poetic use), and بماند is a past/stative verb with the archaic prefixed ب- marking the action 'remained'. Word order places the locative first, then subject and temporal phrase, finishing with the verb.
segments.s509-g2: پس‌انگه is an adverb 'then/afterwards'. بجنبید is an older/past form of جنبیدن with a prefixed ب-; here it means 'moved/was roused'. The conjunction و links to لشکر براند, where براند (from راندن) is a past/action verb 'drove/put in motion' taking لشکر 'the army' as its object. The clause thus says he moved and set the army moving.
segments.s509-g3: ازان سبز دریا = 'from that green sea' (ازان = 'from that'). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when', and گشتند is the past plural of گشتن used here as 'they turned/they went back'. باز after the verb emphasises 'again/returned' or marks the motion back. The clause sets the time/place of the following action.
segments.s509-g4: بیابان گرفتند is an idiomatic past-plural clause 'they took to the desert/wilderness' where گرفتند (past of گرفتن) means 'took' or 'seized' in the sense 'took up a course'. The coordinating و links راه دراز as an appositive/continuation: 'and (took) a long road' — راه دراز lacks an explicit verb and is understood as the object/route they adopted.
segments.s509-g5: چو منزل به منزل = 'when, from stage to stage' (repetitive adverbial phrase showing successive stages). به حلوان رسید places the goal at the end with رسید 'arrived/reached' (past), the verb agreeing implicitly with the same plural subject 'they'. The sequence emphasises progressive travel until arrival at Halwan.
segments.s509-g6: یکی = 'a certain' or 'one', introducing an item encountered. مایه‌ور is an adjective 'wealthy/rich', and باره (here) is a 'load' or 'laden (caravan)'. The conjunction و links شهر 'the town', and دید is the past verb 'they saw'. The phrase lists what they beheld upon arrival: a rich laden caravan and the town.
segments.s509-g7: به پیش آمدندش = 'came before him / came to his presence': به پیش is a fronting/adverbial phrase 'to the front/in presence of', آمدندش is the past plural verb آمدند with the enclitic -ش 'to him'. بزرگان شهر is the subject/group 'the great men (elders) of the city' placed after the verbal phrase for emphasis.
segments.s509-g8: کسی کش = 'one person' with کش functioning as an agentive/formative element implying 'one notable for ...'; ز here is the poetic preposition 'for/on account of'. نام و خرد 'name (i.e. fame) and wisdom' are the qualities mentioned, بود is the past copula 'was', and بهر is the preposition 'for/toward' used idiomatically to mark purpose/role. The whole segment states that among them was a man notable for fame and wisdom.

sentences.s509-s37: At nightfall the king of Yemen returned; the whole host resounded with shouts.
sentences.s509-s21: They took from them many captives — women and children, the small and the young, and the old.
sentences.s509-s22: He came toward Nimruz and closed the road; he washed the whole face of the world of the enemy.
sentences.s509-s23: From that place he set out toward Yemen — the sovereign, accompanied by a renowned company.
sentences.s509-s24: When the king of Yemen heard, he came with his nobles to the king of the world.
sentences.s509-s25: He selected many gifts from Yemen — valuable and beautiful, just as was fitting.
sentences.s509-s26: He loaded ten camels from the spoils of Yemen; then he loaded another five with dinars.
sentences.s509-s27: He then loaded another ten camels with dirhams; when there is money, the heart has no sorrow.
sentences.s509-s28: Another gift: a thousand measures of saffron, and countless brocades and garments.
sentences.s509-s13: Leading the Sindians was a man called Bandah — a proud horseman, with counsel and will.
sentences.s509-s14: One man who had joined the fight was cast down; he fell to the ground like a mountain.
sentences.s509-s15: Night fell on that Sindh plain; no Sindhi remained. Alexander drove his army in pursuit.
sentences.s509-s16: Into his hands came eighty-five elephants, and likewise the golden crown, a sword, and treasure.
sentences.s509-s29: There was one cup of peridot in the treasure; in that (cup) were seventy‑five uncut (gems).
sentences.s509-s30: Another cup was of lapis lazuli; in it he placed sixty yellow rubies.
sentences.s509-s31: From the red ruby, from its cut, ten gems — he gave them to the obedient and uttered praise.
sentences.s509-s32: They reached before the king's inner curtain, with gifts and with offerings.
sentences.s509-s9: When the king heard the speaker's words, he set out from Ḥalwān toward Sind with an army.
sentences.s509-s10: The horsemen of Sind received him; an ally for that very battle came from India.
sentences.s509-s11: Whoever, at a sudden outset, was heart-weary, had washed his hands of shedding blood.
sentences.s509-s12: They brought elephants and wealthy Indians; a roar arose and the trumpets’ lament was heard.
sentences.s509-s17: Women, children, and old men went on the road, weeping, toward the king.
sentences.s509-s18: O alert king, with counsel and intelligence, take counsel about this land, and do not bring harm upon it.
sentences.s509-s19: For the outcome of your day will also pass; may it be that the world does not entrust (it) to the wicked.
sentences.s509-s20: Alexander did not bring them affection; to the weary he did not even show a face.
sentences.s509-s33: Alexander asked, music was played for them, and he recognised them near the throne.
sentences.s509-s34: The king of Yemen cried 'Bravo — go!' and said, 'May you be victorious in the assembly.'
sentences.s509-s35: I am glad for you if you stay here two months; you will be spared the road of the king and the army.
sentences.s509-s36: Alexander cried 'Bravo — go!' and said, 'May wisdom always be your companion.'
sentences.s509-s5: They went with gifts and offerings — carrying sweetmeats and tributes — the chiefs up to the presence of the king.
sentences.s509-s6: Alexander took up a light/trifling question and asked, 'What wondrous thing do you see here?'
sentences.s509-s7: The speaker said to him, 'O king, we do not know of anything that will be of use.'
sentences.s509-s8: On this frontier there is dervishhood and hardship; if you pass this, may (it) remain in your hand.
sentences.s509-s1: At that place the king remained for a month; then he moved and set the army in motion.
sentences.s509-s2: When they turned back from that green sea, they took to the wilderness and a long road.
sentences.s509-s3: When, stage by stage, they reached Halwan, they saw a certain wealthy laden caravan and the town.
sentences.s509-s4: The great men of the city came before him; there was one man among them famed for renown and wisdom.
